quiz_questions.question_text: What are the three fundamental dimensions used in mechanics?

options.text: Force, Energy, and Power
Speed, Acceleration, and Velocity
Mass [M], Length [L], and Time [T]
Meter, Kilogram, and Second

quiz_questions.explanation: The fundamental dimensions in mechanics are mass [M], length [L], and time [T]. All mechanical quantities can be expressed as combinations of these three dimensions.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the dimension of velocity?

options.text: [L T⁻²]
[L T⁻¹]
[M L T⁻¹]
[L²T⁻¹]

quiz_questions.explanation: Velocity = displacement / time, so its dimension is [L] / [T] = [L T⁻¹]. Direction is not represented in dimensional analysis.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the dimension of acceleration?

options.text: [L T⁻¹]
[M T⁻²]
[M L T⁻²]
[L T⁻²]

quiz_questions.explanation: Acceleration = change in velocity / time = [L T⁻¹] / [T] = [L T⁻²].

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the dimension of force?

options.text: [M L T⁻¹]
[M L² T⁻²]
[M L T⁻²]
[L T⁻²]

quiz_questions.explanation: Force = mass × acceleration = [M] × [L T⁻²] = [M L T⁻²]. This is the dimension of the newton (N = kg·m/s²).

quiz_questions.question_text: Can you add a quantity with dimension [M] to a quantity with dimension [L]?

options.text: Yes — all physical quantities can be combined
Only if you convert units first
Yes — the result takes the dimension of the larger quantity
No — only quantities with the same dimensions can be added or subtracted

quiz_questions.explanation: Dimensional homogeneity requires that all terms being added or subtracted have identical dimensions. Adding mass to length is dimensionally inconsistent and physically meaningless.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the dimension of energy (or work)?

options.text: [M L T⁻²]
[M L T⁻¹]
[M L² T⁻²]
[L² T⁻²]

quiz_questions.explanation: Energy = force × distance = [M L T⁻²] × [L] = [M L² T⁻²]. This is the dimension of the joule (J = kg·m²/s²).

quiz_questions.question_text: What is dimensional analysis used for?

options.text: Checking whether equations are dimensionally consistent and converting units
Replacing numerical calculations in physics
Measuring the dimensions of physical objects
Creating new physical quantities

quiz_questions.explanation: Dimensional analysis verifies that equations are dimensionally consistent (both sides have the same dimensions) and helps convert between units. It can also help derive relationships between physical quantities.

quiz_questions.question_text: An equation has [M L T⁻²] on the left side and [M L² T⁻³] on the right side. Is this equation valid?

options.text: No — the dimensions on both sides must match for the equation to be valid
Yes — close enough is acceptable in dimensional analysis
Yes — the exponents only differ by one
Cannot determine without numerical values

quiz_questions.explanation: For an equation to be dimensionally valid, every term on both sides must have identical dimensions. [M L T⁻²] ≠ [M L² T⁻³], so the equation is dimensionally incorrect.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the dimension of power?

options.text: [M L² T⁻²]
[M L² T⁻³]
[L² T⁻³]
[M L T⁻²]

quiz_questions.explanation: Power = energy / time = [M L² T⁻²] / [T] = [M L² T⁻³]. This is the dimension of the watt (W = kg·m²/s³).

quiz_questions.question_text: What dimension does a dimensionless quantity have?

options.text: Dimensionless quantities don't exist in physics
[1] or no dimensions — it is a pure number
[M⁰ L⁰ T⁰] which equals [M L T]
It has the dimensions of its largest component

quiz_questions.explanation: A dimensionless quantity has no dimensions (all exponents are zero). Examples include the coefficient of friction, angle in radians, and the refractive index. It is a pure number.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes dimensional analysis?

options.text: A method of checking the validity of physics equations by ensuring that all terms have the same fundamental dimensions
A way to measure the three dimensions of an object
A technique for calculating exact numerical answers
A method for determining the units of measuring instruments

quiz_questions.explanation: Dimensional analysis examines the fundamental dimensions [M], [L], [T], etc. on both sides of an equation. If dimensions don't match, the equation must contain an error.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes the dimension of a physical quantity?

options.text: The SI unit used to measure the quantity
The expression of the quantity in terms of fundamental dimensions (M, L, T, etc.) that describes what type of quantity it represents
The number of significant figures in the measurement
The physical size of the quantity

quiz_questions.explanation: The dimension tells you the fundamental nature of a quantity. For example, both speed (m/s) and velocity (m/s) have the dimension [L T⁻¹], telling us they are fundamentally the same type of quantity.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes dimensional homogeneity?

options.text: The requirement that every additive term in a valid physics equation must have the same dimensions
The requirement that all numbers be of similar size
The property that all vectors have the same direction
The condition that all measurements use the same units

quiz_questions.explanation: Dimensional homogeneity means that in an equation like v = u + at, each term (v, u, and at) must have the same dimensions. This is a necessary (but not sufficient) condition for an equation to be correct.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes a dimensionless quantity?

options.text: A quantity that has no numerical value
A pure number that has no physical dimensions, formed when all dimensional powers cancel to zero
A quantity that is too small to measure
A quantity that exists only in mathematics

quiz_questions.explanation: Dimensionless quantities are pure numbers. Angles in radians, coefficients (like friction), and ratios of like quantities are dimensionless. Dimensional analysis cannot determine their value.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes the Buckingham Pi theorem?

options.text: A method for converting between imperial and metric units
A theorem stating that any physically meaningful equation involving n variables and k fundamental dimensions can be rewritten using n − k independent dimensionless groups
A rule for counting significant figures in products
A formula for calculating the value of π in different unit systems

quiz_questions.explanation: The Buckingham Pi theorem is a powerful tool in dimensional analysis. It states that the physics of a problem can be captured by a set of dimensionless groups (Pi groups), reducing the number of variables that need to be studied.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes a conversion factor in dimensional analysis?

options.text: The error in a dimensional calculation
A multiplicative factor with dimensions that, when applied to a measurement, changes its units while preserving the physical quantity
A dimensionless number used to scale an equation
The ratio of the largest to smallest measurement

quiz_questions.explanation: Conversion factors like (1 km / 1000 m) have dimensions and value of 1, allowing unit changes without changing the physical quantity. They are the practical application of dimensional analysis.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes the principle of dimensional consistency?

options.text: The rule that dimensions must always be integers
The fundamental rule that a valid equation must have identical dimensions on both sides and in every additive term
The principle that larger dimensions are more important
The idea that all measurements should use SI units

quiz_questions.explanation: Dimensional consistency is a necessary condition for any valid physical equation. If the dimensions don't match, the equation is guaranteed to be wrong. However, dimensional consistency alone doesn't guarantee correctness.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes a derived dimension?

options.text: A dimension formed by combining fundamental dimensions through multiplication, division, or exponentiation
A dimension that was discovered after the original seven
A dimension that exists only in theory
A dimension that varies with temperature

quiz_questions.explanation: Derived dimensions are built from fundamental ones. Velocity [L T⁻¹], force [M L T⁻²], and energy [M L² T⁻²] are all derived dimensions formed by combining [M], [L], and [T].

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes dimensional formula?

options.text: The units of a measuring device
The chemical formula for a substance
An expression showing how a physical quantity is composed of the fundamental dimensions, written using dimensional symbols with exponents
The mathematical equation relating two quantities

quiz_questions.explanation: A dimensional formula expresses a quantity in terms of fundamental dimensions. For example, the dimensional formula of force is [M¹ L¹ T⁻²], showing that force involves mass, length, and the inverse square of time.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes unit analysis (also called factor-label method)?

options.text: A technique for determining significant figures
A problem-solving technique that tracks units through a calculation using conversion factors to ensure the final answer has the correct units
A way to classify physical quantities as scalar or vector
A method of labeling measuring instruments

quiz_questions.explanation: Unit analysis (factor-label method) systematically converts units by multiplying by conversion factors arranged so that unwanted units cancel and desired units remain. It guarantees correct unit outcomes.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student proposes that the period T of a simple pendulum depends on its length L and gravitational acceleration g. Using dimensional analysis, she finds T = k√(L/g). Why can't dimensional analysis determine the constant k?

options.text: Because the formula is incorrect
Because k is dimensionless — dimensional analysis only ensures dimensional consistency and cannot determine pure numerical constants
Because k depends on the mass of the pendulum
Because k changes with every pendulum

quiz_questions.explanation: Since k is a dimensionless number (in this case 2π), it contributes no dimensions. Dimensional analysis can only match dimensions, not determine dimensionless constants. Experiment or derivation reveals k = 2π.

quiz_questions.question_text: A physics student writes: F = mv² (force equals mass times velocity squared). Use dimensional analysis to check this claim.

options.text: Incorrect — [F] = [M L T⁻²] but [mv²] = [M L² T⁻²], which is the dimension of energy, not force
Incorrect — you cannot square a vector quantity
Cannot be checked because velocity is a vector
Correct — the dimensions match on both sides

quiz_questions.explanation: [F] = [M L T⁻²]. [mv²] = [M][L T⁻¹]² = [M L² T⁻²]. Since [M L T⁻²] ≠ [M L² T⁻²], the equation is dimensionally inconsistent. The correct relationship requires dividing by a length: F = mv²/r (centripetal force).

quiz_questions.question_text: An engineer needs to know how drag force F depends on fluid density ρ, velocity v, and cross-sectional area A. Using dimensions [F] = [M L T⁻²], [ρ] = [M L⁻³], [v] = [L T⁻¹], [A] = [L²], what combination works?

options.text: F ∝ ρvA — this gives [M L⁻¹ T⁻¹] which matches force
F ∝ ρv²A — checking: [M L⁻³][L T⁻¹]²[L²] = [M L⁻³][L² T⁻²][L²] = [M L T⁻²] ✓
F ∝ ρv³A² — more v's and A's mean more force
Dimensional analysis cannot determine this relationship

quiz_questions.explanation: By matching dimensions: [M L⁻³]ᵃ[L T⁻¹]ᵇ[L²]ᶜ = [M L T⁻²]. Solving: a = 1, b = 2, c = 1. So F ∝ ρv²A. The actual drag equation is F = ½CᴅρAv², where ½Cᴅ is the dimensionless drag coefficient.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student derives an equation and gets: x = ½at² + vt + F where x is position, a is acceleration, t is time, v is velocity, and F is force. Is this equation valid?

options.text: Yes — all five variables are valid physical quantities
Yes — the equation uses correct physics symbols
No — the term F has dimension [M L T⁻²] while the other terms have dimension [L], violating dimensional homogeneity
Cannot be determined without numerical values

quiz_questions.explanation: Checking each term: [½at²] = [L T⁻²][T²] = [L] ✓, [vt] = [L T⁻¹][T] = [L] ✓, [F] = [M L T⁻²] ✗. The force term has different dimensions from the position terms, so the equation is invalid.

quiz_questions.question_text: A researcher measures the speed of a car as 90 km/h and needs it in m/s. She uses dimensional analysis (factor-label method) to convert. What is the correct setup?

options.text: 90 km/h ÷ 3.6 is correct but dimensional analysis isn't needed
90 km/h × (1000 m / 1 km) × (1 h / 3600 s) = 25 m/s — conversion factors cancel unwanted units

quiz_questions.explanation: The factor-label method: 90 (km/h) × (1000 m/1 km) × (1 h/3600 s). The km cancels, the h cancels, leaving m/s: 90 × 1000/3600 = 25 m/s. Each conversion factor equals 1, preserving the physical quantity.

quiz_questions.question_text: A physics equation states: P = ρgh, where P is pressure, ρ is density, g is gravitational acceleration, and h is height. Verify this equation using dimensional analysis.

options.text: Invalid — pressure has no mass dimension
Valid — [ρgh] = [M L⁻³][L T⁻²][L] = [M L⁻¹ T⁻²] = [P] (pressure = force/area = [M L T⁻²]/[L²]) ✓
Valid — but only when h is measured in meters
Invalid — density has the wrong dimensions for a pressure equation

quiz_questions.explanation: [P] = [Force/Area] = [M L T⁻²]/[L²] = [M L⁻¹ T⁻²]. [ρgh] = [M L⁻³][L T⁻²][L] = [M L⁻³⁺¹⁺¹ T⁻²] = [M L⁻¹ T⁻²] ✓. The dimensions match, confirming the equation is dimensionally valid.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two students argue about the equation E = mc. Student A says it should be E = mc². Student B argues both could be correct 'depending on the system.' Who is right?

options.text: Student B — any equation can be correct in some system of units
Neither — E = mc³ is the correct equation
Student A — dimensional analysis proves E = mc is wrong: [M][L T⁻¹] = [M L T⁻¹] ≠ [M L² T⁻²] = [Energy]. Only E = mc² is dimensionally correct
Both are wrong — energy cannot be related to mass

quiz_questions.explanation: [E] = [M L² T⁻²]. [mc] = [M][L T⁻¹] = [M L T⁻¹] — wrong dimensions. [mc²] = [M][L T⁻¹]² = [M L² T⁻²] — matches energy. The equation E = mc has no system where it could work because the dimensions are fundamentally mismatched.

quiz_questions.question_text: A fluid dynamics student wants to determine if a new equation v = √(2P/ρ) is plausible, where v is velocity, P is pressure, and ρ is density. Check using dimensional analysis.

options.text: Plausible — [2P/ρ]^½ = ([M L⁻¹ T⁻²]/[M L⁻³])^½ = [L² T⁻²]^½ = [L T⁻¹] = [v] ✓
Plausible — but only if ρ is in g/cm³
Implausible — the square root changes the dimensions
Implausible — pressure and density can't be combined this way

quiz_questions.explanation: [P/ρ] = [M L⁻¹ T⁻²] / [M L⁻³] = [L² T⁻²]. Taking the square root: [L² T⁻²]^½ = [L T⁻¹] = dimensions of velocity ✓. The equation is dimensionally valid (it's actually Torricelli-type relation).

quiz_questions.question_text: An airplane designer proposes that the lift force L depends on air density ρ, wing area A, and velocity v as L = ρA²v. Dimensional analysis reveals this is wrong. What should the correct dependence be?

options.text: L ∝ ρ²Av — double the density term
L ∝ ρAv² — checking: [M L⁻³][L²][L T⁻¹]² = [M L⁻³][L²][L² T⁻²] = [M L T⁻²] = [Force] ✓
L ∝ ρA²v² — just square everything
L ∝ √(ρAv) — take the square root

quiz_questions.explanation: Checking the proposed L = ρA²v: [M L⁻³][L²]²[L T⁻¹] = [M L⁻³][L⁴][L T⁻¹] = [M L² T⁻¹] ≠ [M L T⁻²]. The correct combination L ∝ ρAv²: [M L⁻³][L²][L² T⁻²] = [M L T⁻²] ✓.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student solves a problem and gets the answer in units of kg·m²/s³. Without knowing what formula was used, what physical quantity does this represent?

options.text: Momentum — this is the product of mass and velocity
Energy — these are the units of joules
Force — kg·m²/s³ is another way to write newtons
Power — [M L² T⁻³] is the dimension of power (watts), which is the rate of energy transfer

quiz_questions.explanation: [M L² T⁻³] = [M L² T⁻²]/[T] = Energy/Time = Power. The unit kg·m²/s³ is equivalent to the watt (W). Dimensional analysis can identify a physical quantity from its dimensions alone.
